paper_title: FrontierSmith: Synthesizing Open-Ended Coding Problems at Scale

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Many real-world coding challenges are open-ended and admit no known optimal solution. Yet, recent progress in LLM coding has focused on well-defined tasks such as feature implementation, bug fixing, and competitive programming. Open-ended coding remains a weak spot for LLMs, largely because open-ended training problems are scarce and expensive to construct. Our goal is to synthesize open-ended coding problems at scale to train stronger LLM coders. We introduce FrontierSmith, an automated system for iteratively evolving open-ended problems from existing closed-ended coding tasks. Starting from competitive programming problems, FrontierSmith generates candidate open-ended variants by changing the problems'goals, restricting outputs, and generalizing inputs. It then uses a quantitative idea divergence metric to select problems that elicit genuinely diverse approaches from different solvers. Agents then generate test cases and verifiers for the surviving candidates. On two open-ended coding benchmarks, training on our synthesized data yields substantial gains over the base models: Qwen3.5-9B improves by +8.82 score on FrontierCS and +306.36 (Elo-rating-based performance) on ALE-bench; Qwen3.5-27B improves by +12.12 and +309.12, respectively. The synthesized problems also make agents take more turns and use more tokens, similar to human-curated ones, suggesting that closed-ended seeds can be a practical starting point for long-horizon coding data.

Gonzalez1, Alvin Cheung1 1UC Berkeley, 2UC San Diego, 3University of Washington, 4Stanford University, 5Princeton University, 6Massachusetts Institute of Technology, 7Bespoke Labs Correspondence: qmang@berkeley.edu, wenhao.chai@princeton.edu Model: Sample Problems & Training Scripts: github.com/FrontierCS/FrontierSmith huggingface.co/runyuanhe/qwen35-9b-frontiersmith

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Many real-world coding challenges are open-ended and admit no known optimal solution. Yet, recent progress in LLM coding has focused on well-defined tasks such as feature implementation, bug fixing, and competitive programming. Open-ended coding remains weak spot for LLMs, largely because open-ended training problems are scarce and expensive to construct. Our goal is to synthesize open-ended coding problems at scale to train stronger LLM coders. We introduce FrontierSmith, an automated system for iteratively evolving open-ended problems from existing closed-ended coding tasks. Starting from competitive programming problems, FrontierSmith generates candidate open-ended variants by changing the problems goals, restricting outputs, and generalizing inputs. It then uses quantitative idea divergence metric to select problems that elicit genuinely diverse approaches from different solvers. Agents then generate test cases and verifiers for the surviving candidates. On two open-ended coding benchmarks, training on our synthesized data yields substantial gains over the base models: Qwen3.5-9B improves by +8.82 score on FrontierCS and +306.36 (Elo-rating-based performance) on ALE-bench; Qwen3.5-27B improves by +12.12 and +309.12, respectively. The synthesized problems also make agents take more turns and use more tokens, similar to human-curated ones, suggesting that closed-ended seeds can be practical starting point for long-horizon coding data.

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: LLMs now excel at well-defined coding tasks, reaching gold-medal performance in competitive programming (ICPC Foundation, 2026) and solving over 80% of SWE-bench verified issues (Jimenez et al., 2024). Yet these settings are mostly closed-ended: correctness is discrete and efficiently verifiable. Open-ended coding tasks, in contrast, lack tractable certificates of optimality at the target scale and score submissions by continuous quality. For example, in cloud cluster scheduling, many job-to-machine assignments are feasible, but they differ continuously in makespan, tail latency, energy use, and utilization; checking feasibility is easy, whereas certifying global optimality at production scale is intractable (Li et al., 2026). This difficulty is reflected in current open-ended benchmarks. On FrontierCS (Mang et al., 2025), human experts score 95.41 on algorithmic tasks, compared with 29.37 for Gemini 3.0 Pro (Google DeepMind, 2025); on ALE-bench (Imajuku et al., 2025), frontier LLMs still trail average human participants in heuristic contests. This gap reflects data asymmetry: verified closed-ended tasks are abundant, while open-ended tasks remain scarce. We aim to automatically synthesize open-ended coding problems at scale to train stronger LLM coders. For closed-ended tasks, the data problem is largely solved. Codeforces (Codeforces, 2026) and LeetCode (LeetCode, 2026) provide hundreds of thousands of closed-ended problems with verified solutions, enabling reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards to drive substantial gains. Open-ended coding has no equivalent. *Equal contribution, Project lead 1 Preprint FrontierCS Team Figure 1 FrontierSmith pipeline. FrontierSmith converts closed-ended coding problems into open-ended ones through mutation, filtering, and automated environment construction. Candidate mugtations are ranked by idea divergence across sampled solutions, then validated by test-case and verifier agents. The resulting problems replace binary pass/fail feedback with continuous scores, enabling scalable training data synthesis for open-ended coding. FrontierCS and ALE-bench, two of the largest open-ended coding benchmarks, contains only around 240 and 40 human-curated problems, respectively. Constructing such problems manually is prohibitively expensive: each requires carefully designed optimization objective, verifier that produces continuous scores rather than binary judgments, and reliable test cases. Beyond engineering cost, expert judgment is needed to verify each problem is genuinely open-ended and not dominated by single strategy (i.e., the core idea shared by class of solutions, regardless of implementation). Recent work has automated coding data synthesis, but is exclusively for closed-ended, binary-correctness settings. Given existing problem statements, several methods generate high-quality test cases at scale (He et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2025c; Liu et al., 2025). Other methods synthesize new problems across competitive programming (Zhou et al., 2025), software engineering (Yang et al., 2025; Badertdinov et al., 2025; Sonwane et al., 2025), terminal environments (Gandhi et al., 2026), and self-play (Zhao et al., 2025; Jana et al., 2026). None of them transfers to open-ended problems, which need novel formulations without known optimum, and way to reliably score solution quality on continuous scale. We build FrontierSmith, an automated system that continuously evolves open-ended coding problems from closed tasks at scale. As shown in Figure 1, starting from closed-end competitive programming problems, which provide large and diverse seed corpus, FrontierSmiths pipeline mutates them into open-ended variants along three axes: changing goals, restricting outputs, and generalizing inputs. Each mutation turns problem with known efficient solution into one where exact solutions are intractable, forcing solvers to adopt diverse strategies. We then quantify the open-endedness of the remaining candidates with novel idea divergence metric, which estimates the probability that two independent solvers use different core algorithms. This estimation proceeds in two stages: first via LLM-based comparison of solution strategies, then via test-score similarity once test cases and verifiers are constructed for each candidate. These are built by separate agents systems that cross-validate each other to ensure correctness. Validated problems then join the seed pool, so each subsequent round draws from an increasingly diverse starting set. We train Qwen3.5-9B and Qwen3.5-27B (Team, 2026) on 200 synthesized problems with GRPO (Shao et al., 2024). The 9B model gains +8.82 score on FrontierCS and +306.36 (Elo-rating-based performance) on ALE-bench; the 27B model gains +12.12 and +309.12, respectively. Against two controls, closed-ended HardTests (He et al., 2025) and random rewards (Shao et al., 2025), our synthetic problems lead by +5.24 / +236.40 and +7.58 / +256.76 on FrontierCS / ALE-bench, confirming that open-ended formulations and genuine reward signals are both necessary. Ablations show the filter is critical: removing it drops performance 2 Preprint FrontierCS Team by 2.05 points on FrontierCS. Beyond training, our idea divergence metric cleanly separates real open-ended problems from competitive programming problems, validating it as problem-quality classifier. Finally, open-ended problems have recently shown to elicit distinctive long-horizon behavior in code agents, including more turns, tool calls, and thinking tokens (Cemri et al., 2026; Liu et al., 2026a; Qu et al., 2026; Liu et al., 2026b); our synthetic problems match these patterns, suggesting they capture the same structure as human-curated ones. In sum, our key findings are: (1) closed-ended problems can serve as seeds for open-ended synthesis via mutations that remove known optima; (2) idea divergence is an effective and tractable signal for selecting high-quality synthetic problems; and (3) FrontierSmith-generated problems yield training gains comparable to human-curated data, and exhibit long-horizon behavior when processed by agents.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Coding data synthesis for closed tasks. Recent work synthesizes coding problems and test infrastructure under binary correctness. AutoCode (Zhou et al., 2025), SWE-smith (Yang et al., 2025), BugPilot (Sonwane et al., 2025), Endless Terminals (Gandhi et al., 2026), GASP (Jana et al., 2026), and SGS (Bailey et al., 2026) synthesize problems; HardTests (He et al., 2025), CodeContests+ (Wang et al., 2025c), rStar-Coder (Liu et al., 2025), SWE-rebench (Badertdinov et al., 2025), and R2E-Gym (Jain et al., 2025) build tests and verifiers; AgentCoder (Huang et al., 2023) and CURE (Wang et al., 2025a) cross-validate code and tests across multiple agents. None produces open-ended problems, the regime FrontierSmith targets. Open-ended evaluation and solution diversity. Open-ended coding benchmarks score solutions on continuous quality scale: FrontierCS (Mang et al., 2025), ALE-bench (Imajuku et al., 2025), HeuriGym (Chen et al., 2025), KernelBench (Ouyang et al., 2025), RE-Bench (Wijk et al., 2024), and MLE-bench (Chan et al., 2024). NP-Engine (Li et al., 2025) hand-crafts instance generators and rule-based verifiers for ten classical NP-hard tasks, training via RLVR with approximation-ratio rewards; its catalog is fixed and only difficulty varies, which caps the diversity of generated problems. Recent work (Novikov et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2025b; Maheswaran et al., 2026) produces frontier results in open-ended mathematics and algorithm design. Our idea divergence metric draws on quality-diversity and novelty search (Lehman and Stanley, 2011; Mouret and Clune, 2015; Wang et al., 2019; Bradley et al., 2024; Faldor et al., 2025; Pourcel et al., 2024), with code-specific analogs in Lee et al. (2025) and Ju et al. (2025). Unlike these works, which measure diversity of one models outputs, we use inter-solver divergence as problem-quality filter that admits only formulations capable of eliciting different core algorithms, necessary but not sufficient signal for open-endedness. Mutation-based synthesis and iterative self-play. Evolving prompts or programs by mutation is established: WizardLM (Xu et al., 2023), WizardCoder (Luo et al., 2024), EvoEval (Xia et al., 2024), and Auto Evol-Instruct (Zeng et al., 2024) mutate seed prompts; FunSearch (Romera-Paredes et al., 2024), AlphaEvolve (Novikov et al., 2025), ELM (Lehman et al., 2022), and EoH (Liu et al., 2024) mutate programs inside evolutionary loops. Iterative bootstrap recycles self-generated data through training rounds: STaR (Zelikman et al., 2022), ReST-EM (Singh et al., 2024), V-STaR (Hosseini et al., 2024), Self-Rewarding LM (Yuan et al., 2024), R-Zero (Huang et al., 2026), EVA (Ye et al., 2024), and Absolute Zero (Zhao et al., 2025). FrontierSmith differs: we mutate problem formulations rather than solutions or instructions, and admit them by inter-solver divergence rather than learning gain, self-consistency, or solver pass rate. These signals reward solver-side progress or confidence rather than the open-endedness of the problem itself.

sections.title: 3.1 Mutation Problem Formulation
sections.content: Existing work on synthesizing closed-ended coding tasks focuses on constructing test environments rather than problem formulations, because closed-ended formulations can be drawn from existing repositories (Zhou 3 Preprint FrontierCS Team Algorithm 1: FrontierSmith: Open-Ended Problem Synthesis Input: Seed pool S, CP corpus D, LLM M, sample size B, solutions per candidate n, budgets Ndiv, Nfinal Output: Validated open-ended problem set D; sample problems from S; Mutate(B, M); CoarseFilter(C, M); foreach candidate do {s1, . . . , sn} draw solutions for from M; ˆd(c) 1 (n 2) i<j LLM-as-a-Judge(si, sj); (cid:80) end topNdiv foreach candidate do (cid:0)C, ˆd()(cid:1); add CP corpus to seed pool mutate goals, outputs, inputs remove non-open-ended idea divergence keep highest divergence (Tc, Vc) BuildAndValidate(c, M); Update ˆd(c) using score vectors qi = (Vc(si, t1), . . . , Vc(si, tm)); test cases + verifier with cross-validation end topNfinal P; 15 16 return (cid:0)C, ˆd()(cid:1); re-rank with verified divergence expand seed pool for next round 1 2 4 5 6 7 8 10 11 12 13 14 et al., 2025; He et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2025c) and domain-specific datasets (Yang et al., 2025; Sonwane et al., 2025). Open-ended problems have no such source; the formulation itself is the central challenge. Our key insight is that closed-ended problems can serve as seeds for synthesizing open-ended ones. Targeted mutations to their formulations can remove known optima while preserving meaningful way to evaluate submission quality. Concretely, we represent problem formulation as tuple (O, CI , CO) where is the computational goal, CI the admissible problem instances, and CO the constraints on valid program outputs. In this representation, can take various forms: required output, decision criterion, property to satisfy, or quantity to optimize. We define three mutation types that transform closed-ended formulation into an open-ended one: 1. Changing goals (O O): replace decision or exact-answer goal with an optimization-oriented goal that admits graded performance. For example, 2-SAT (Aspvall et al., 1979) decides whether Boolean formula with two-literal clauses is satisfiable; mutation of this goal produces Min-True 2-SAT (Gusfield and Pitt, 1992), which keeps the same input and output but asks for satisfying assignment that minimizes the number of true variables. 2. Restricting outputs (CO O): add or tighten constraints on valid outputs while keeping the underlying goal fixed. For example, the minimum spanning tree problem (Kruskal, 1956) on weighted graph asks for tree connecting all vertices with minimum total edge weight, which admits greedy solution. Adding per-vertex degree bounds yields the NP-hard degree-constrained spanning tree (Narula and Ho, 1980). At scale, exact solutions become infeasible, and different approximation strategies yield solutions of varying quality. 3. Generalizing inputs (CI I ): relax structural assumptions on the input domain while keeping the goal and output constraints fixed. For example, the maximum independent set asks for the largest vertex set with no two vertices sharing an edge. On bipartite graphs, it is polynomial via Kőnigs theorem (König, 1990), but on arbitrary graphs it becomes one of Karps original NP-complete problems (Karp, 2009), similarly making exact solutions infeasible at scale. In all three mutation types, the resulting problem has no efficient method to certify its optimum at scale and admits continuous quality measure: both conditions for open-endedness. We implement each mutation by prompting an LLM with the seed problem and the desired mutation type; multiple types can apply simultaneously to single candidate. Given only the problem statement, first extracts the original formulation (O, CI , CO) and then produces an open-ended candidate (O, , O). 4 Preprint FrontierCS Team Problem LLM Solvers solutions {s1, . . . , s6} greedy dynamic programming local search gradient descent LLM-as-a-judge-based estimation Execution-based estimation s1 s2 s3 s4 s5 s6 t1 t2 t3 tm s1 s2 s3 s4 s6 different same 1 c a e 0 s1 s2 s3 s4 s6 q1 q2 q3 q4 q5 q6 ˆd(c) = avg LLM-as-a-Judge(si, sj) ˆd(c) = avg 1 qi qj2 LLM-based estimation. An LLM judge labels each solution pair as sameor different-strategy. ˆd(c) is the fraction of pairs judged different. Blue cells: different-strategy; gray cells: same-strategy. Execution-grounded estimation. Each solution is run on the test cases t1, . . . , tm and scored by the verifier, yielding score vector qi. ˆd(c) is the average pairwise distance between score vectors. Figure 2 Estimating idea divergence ˆd(c), the probability that two independently sampled LLM-generated solutions to candidate problem use different algorithmic strategies. We draw 6 solutions s1, . . . , s6 from an LLM solver and estimate ˆd(c) in two complementary ways. The shape attached to each si denotes its underlying algorithmic strategy.

sections.title: 3.2 Problem Filtering
sections.content: Mutation produces broad but noisy pool; we apply two filters in sequence to retain only high-quality open-ended candidates. Coarse LLM-as-a-judge filter. The first filter is coarse LLM-as-a-judge check. We prompt to check three conditions: the problem defines an optimization objective with no known optimum; multiple distinct strategies are plausible; and scoring function can meaningfully rank submissions. Candidates failing any condition are discarded. Idea divergence filter. The coarse filter removes closed-ended candidates but does not measure solution diversity. If one strategy dominates, the problem effectively degenerates to closed-ended task. Well-designed open-ended settings exhibit genuine solution diversity. The AtCoder Heuristic Contest (Imajuku et al., 2025) and the database join-ordering problem (Steinbrunn et al., 1997) illustrate this: top performers explore fundamentally different ideas rather than refine one dominant approach. This diversity also strengthens RL training: under GRPO (Shao et al., 2024), varied strategies that yield meaningfully different rewards produce stronger gradient signal than samples following the same heuristic (Wang et al., 2026). We therefore introduce an idea divergence filter that directly quantifies this diversity. Definition. For candidate problem = (O, CI , CO), we define idea divergence as the probability that two independently generated solutions use different algorithmic strategies (Figure 2): (cid:2)strategy(si) = strategy(sj)(cid:3). d(c) := Psi,sj Solver(c) (1) Solver(c) is the distribution over LLM-generated solutions for c, and strategy() maps each solution to its core algorithmic idea. Computing d(c) exactly is intractable; we provide two complementary estimates below. LLM-based estimate. We draw independent solutions s1, . . . , sn Solver(c). An LLM-as-a-judge labels each pair (si, sj) as sameor different-strategy: ˆd(c) := 1 (cid:1) (cid:0)n 2 (cid:88) i<j LLM-as-a-Judge(cid:0)si, sj (cid:1). (2) 5 Preprint FrontierCS Team where each judge call returns 1 if the strategies differ and 0 otherwise. naive implementation requires O(n2) judge calls. We batch the calls instead, scoring all pairs in small group per query and averaging across groups. Execution-grounded estimate. After testing infrastructure (3.3), we complement with an execution-based estimate. Given test cases Tc = {t1, . . . , tm} and verifier Vc for c, the score vector qi = (Vc(si, t1), . . . , Vc(si, tm)) records sis per-test-case performance, and we estimate divergence as ˆd(c) := 1 (cid:1) (cid:0)n 2 (cid:88) i<j 1 qi qj2 . (3) Selection. The two estimates above are complementary. The LLM-based estimate captures semantic differences in algorithmic strategy, e.g., greedy vs. dynamic programming. The execution-based estimate captures behavioral differences across test cases, e.g., two similar solutions that trade speed against accuracy. Together they form two-stage funnel (Algorithm 1): the LLM-based estimate is applied first since it requires no test infrastructure, retaining the top Ndiv candidates. After testing infrastructure (3.3), the execution-based estimate refines the ranking, and the top Nfinal are selected as the final problem set P.

sections.title: 3.3 Testing Infrastructure
sections.content: Test case generation. For each surviving candidate c, test case agent generates set of inputs Tc (Figure 3). Closed-ended test cases target corner cases and boundary conditions; open-ended test cases must stresstest different algorithmic strategies. Following prior work on test synthesis (Zhou et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2025c), we prompt the agent to write test-case generator programs that produce inputs of varying size and structure, such as sparse versus dense graphs or uniform versus skewed distributions. The testcase agent also receives the solutions sampled by the divergence estimate of 3.2, allowing it to craft adversarial inputs that expose where specific strategies break down. Problem Solutions si Test Case Agent Verifier Agent invalid Tc Vc errors Run si on Tc, score with Vc Validated (Tc, Vc) Figure 3 Test case and verifier synthesis. test case agent produces inputs Tc; verifier agent produces scoring program Vc. Sampled solutions are run on both; each agent uses the others output to validate its own (dashed), iterating until consistent. Verifier generation. Each candidates objective orders solutions but yields no bounded score. separate verifier agent translates into scoring program Vc that returns normalized score in [0, 1]. One common approach is to normalize against baseline solution s: Vc returns 0 when fails to improve on and value in [0, 1) otherwise, scaling with the size of the improvement. The baseline need not be strong; even greedy heuristic or random valid solution suffices. Note that we can ensure O(, t) > 0 by adding constant offset. We assume O(, t) > 0. Letting σO = +1 for maximization objectives and 1 for minimization, (cid:18) Vc(s, t) = max 0, σO O(s, t) O(s, t) max{O(s, t), O(s, t)} (cid:19) . (4) Solutions that crash, time out, or produce unparseable output receive score of 0. Cross-validation protocol. The test case agent and the verifier agent use each others output for selfif solution crashes or validation. The test case agent runs the sampled solutions through the verifier: produces unparseable output on test input, that input is invalid, and the test-case agent revises it. The verifier agent scores the sampled solutions from 3.2 on the generated test cases: if scores collapse to narrow band or contradict the relative quality of solutions, the scoring program is flawed, and the agent revises 6 Preprint FrontierCS Team it. Each agent iterates until the others output no longer exposes errors in its own; candidates that fail to converge within fixed number of rounds are discarded. In our experiments, 10% of candidates that enter this stage produce validated (Tc, Vc) pair, which then feeds the execution-based idea-divergence re-ranking of 3.2.

sections.title: 4.1 Experimental Setup
sections.content: Benchmarks. We evaluate on two open-ended coding benchmarks. FrontierCS (Mang et al., 2025) contains 240 open-ended problems across two tracks. We use the 172 algorithmic problems, which require only local computation; the remaining 68 problems require cloud infrastructure (e.g., GPUs, external services) and are excluded. Submissions are scored on continuous 0100 scale. ALE-bench (Imajuku et al., 2025) draws tasks from AtCoder Heuristic Contests and evaluates submissions using performance-based ratings. We use the ALE-bench-lite subset (10 tasks) for evaluation. Baselines. We compare five configurations: (1) Base: the pretrained model without RL; (2) FrontierCS: RL on 172 human-curated FrontierCS algorithmic problems; (3) ALE-bench: RL on 40 ALE-bench tasks, with rewards computed from each problems public test set. (4) HardTests: RL on 200 problems randomly sampled from the 47,136 closed-ended competitive programming problems from HardTests (He et al., 2025), trained with binary rewards (1 if all test cases pass, 0 otherwise), following the standard competitive programming setup (Codeforces, 2026; ICPC Foundation, 2026); (5) Random Reward: RL on 172 FrontierCS algorithmic problems with the raw rewards drawn from U[0, 100]. Following Shao et al. (2025), this is spurious reward control: it tests whether gains require task-specific reward signals or arise from RL dynamics under uninformative rewards. Problem synthesis. We run Algorithm 1 for 4 iterations. Each iteration samples = 1,000 seed problems from the pool (initialized with HardTests (He et al., 2025)) with Ndiv = 100 and Nfinal = 50, yielding 50 synthetic problems. We use GPT-5.4 Thinking (OpenAI, 2026a) with medium thinking effort for problem formulation mutation (3.1), the coarse LLM-as-a-judge filter, the LLM-based divergence estimate (3.2), and Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Anthropic, 2026) with default thinking effort for sampling solutions (n = 10 per candidate), test case generation, and verifier generation (3.3). The generated problems follow the FrontierCS (Mang et al., 2025) format, and we reuse its evaluation sandbox to execute code and run verifiers. Training configurations. We use veRL (Sheng et al., 2024) with GRPO, learning rate is set to 106 for Qwen3.5-9B and 5 107 for Qwen3.5-27B, rollout batch size 8, and group size 8. We train for 100 steps and evaluate every 10 steps. Qwen3.5-9B trains on 8 A100 GPUs for 1.5 days with maximum response length 16,000 tokens; Qwen3.5-27B trains on 32 H200 GPUs for 1.5 days with maximum response length 32,000 tokens. For the 27B model, we only report Base, FrontierCS, HardTests, and FrontierSmith due to our computing budget.

sections.title: 4.2 Main Results
sections.content: Table 1 reports the main results. FrontierSmith achieves competitive or superior performance to human-curated training data on both benchmarks, and the gains hold across model sizes (9B and 27B), suggesting that our synthesis pipeline scales with model capacity. FrontierSmith vs. human-curated open-ended data. On Qwen3.5-9B, FrontierSmith achieves 10.62 Avg@5 on FrontierCS, close to 11.17 from human-curated FrontierCS problems, gap of only 0.55 points. On ALE-bench, FrontierSmith achieves the highest Best@5 (782.30) across all configurations, and its Avg@5 (633.58) surpasses the FrontierCS-trained baseline (558.49), trailing only the ALE-bench-trained model (657.40) which benefits from in-domain data. On Qwen3.5-27B, FrontierSmith outperforms the human-curated baseline on both benchmarks: FrontierCS (19.82 vs. 13.98) and ALE-bench (661.64 vs. 543.80), improving from base 7 Preprint FrontierCS Team Qwen3.5-9B Qwen3.5-27B

We train for 100 steps and evaluate every 10 steps; reported numbers are the best checkpoint for each metric. Avg@5 and Best@5: average and maximum score over 5 samples per problem. Parentheses: number of training problems. Bold: best; underline: second best. Red cells: training data overlaps with the evaluation benchmark. Figure 4 Test performance over training steps on FrontierCS and ALE-bench (Qwen3.5-9B, 200 problems each). FrontierSmith and HardTests are the same runs as Table 1; No Filter skips both filters. FrontierSmith consistently outperforms the no-filter ablation and the HardTests baseline. of 7.70 and 352.52. These results suggest that automated synthesis can largely substitute for expensive human curation, with strong cross-benchmark generalization. FrontierSmith vs. closed-ended data. HardTests problems serve as the seed corpus for FrontierSmiths mutations, yet training directly on them yields only 5.38 on FrontierCS and 397.18 on ALE-bench for the 9B model, well below FrontierSmiths 10.62 and 633.58. The same pattern holds for the 27B model (11.20 vs. 19.82 on FrontierCS). This demonstrates that mutation transforms closed-ended problems into more effective open-ended training data. Random reward control. The random reward baseline scores 3.04 on FrontierCS and 376.82 on ALE-bench, which is similar to the untrained base (1.80 and 327.22). This rules out problem-format exposure as the source of gains, consistent with Shao et al. (2025).

sections.title: 4.3 Filter Analysis
sections.content: Figure 4 shows test performance over training steps for three 200-problem configurations: FrontierSmith and HardTests are the same runs as in Table 1; No Filter skips both filters and directly builds test infrastructure 8 Preprint FrontierCS Team Figure 5 Comparison of four problem sources. Color encodes the problem source in both panels. Left: Idea divergence cleanly separates open-ended from closed-ended problems. Right: Points show source-agent geometric means of turns and tokens; FrontierSmith and human-curated open-ended problems elicit increasing horizon agent behavior. for 200 mutated problems. On FrontierCS, FrontierSmith reaches 10.62 Avg@5, compared with 8.57 for the no-filter variant. The same trend holds on ALE-bench, where FrontierSmith achieves 633.6 versus 564.4. These results suggest that idea-divergence filtering improves both in-domain performance and cross-benchmark generalization. Both filtered and unfiltered synthetic data outperform the closed-ended HardTests baseline (5.38 and 397.2). We next validate the two filters individually. Coarse filter validation. To validate the coarse LLM-as-a-judge filter (3.2), we apply it to 100 closedended competitive programming problems from HardTests (He et al., 2025) using GPT-5.4 Thinking (OpenAI, 2026a) as the judge. Since all problems in this set are closed-ended, any retained candidate is false positive. The filter rejects 91 and retains 9, yielding false-positive rate of 9%. Conversely, applying the filter to 100 FrontierCS problems rejects 19, false-negative rate of 19%. This is acceptable because the filter is designed to preserve high-quality open-ended problems rather than maximizing recall. Idea divergence as classifier. To test whether idea divergence separates open-ended from closed-ended problems, we sample 10 tasks from each of four sources: FrontierCS (human-curated), FrontierSmith (synthetic), HardTests (closed-ended, sampled from the hardest difficulty tier in human competitive programming contests), and ALE-bench. For each task, we sample n=10 solutions using Claude Sonnet 4.6 and compute the two divergence estimates (3.2). The left panel of Figure 5 shows the results. Using the LLM-based estimate, the three open-ended sources (FrontierCS, FrontierSmith, and ALE-bench) all score around 0.4, roughly 3 higher than HardTests (0.14). FrontierSmith scores slightly higher than the human-curated FrontierCS (0.42 vs. 0.40), suggesting that our filtering pipeline selects problems with genuine solution diversity comparable to the human-curated ones. The execution-grounded estimate shows similar trend: HardTests remains lowest (0.08), while FrontierCS, FrontierSmith, and ALE-bench score 0.11, 0.14, and 0.24, respectively. Although the margin between HardTests and the open-ended sources is smaller, the separation remains clear.

sections.title: 4.4 Long-Horizon Code Agent Behavior
sections.content: Open-ended problems often lead to extended interactions: agents benefit from iterating on solutions, running tests, and refining strategies over many turns. Using the same 40 tasks from 4.3, we run three code agents via the Harbor evaluation framework (Harbor Framework Team, 2026): Claude SDK with Sonnet 4.6 (Anthropic, 2026), Codex with GPT-5.5 (OpenAI, 2026b), and Kimi Code with K2.6 (Moonshot AI, 2026). The right panel of Figure 5 plots geometric-mean turns and tokens for each source-agent pair. ALE-bench drives all three agents into the long-horizon regime, where every pair exceeds 100 turns and 3106 tokens. FrontierSmith exhibits the same long-horizon behavior, with Claude SDK reaching 113 turns and 6.3106 tokens, on par with ALE-bench. HardTests and FrontierCS stay in the short-horizon regime. 9 Preprint

sections.title: 5 Discussion
sections.content: FrontierCS Team Limitations. Our current pipeline operates entirely within self-contained algorithmic environments: each problem takes text input and produces program output, with no external dependencies. This excludes repo-level open-ended tasks that require complex environment setup, such as system optimization with cloud infrastructure, GPU kernel tuning (Cao et al., 2026), or multi-file software engineering. Extending FrontierSmith to these settings would require generating not only problem formulations and verifiers but also reproducible execution environments. Additionally, due to compute budget constraints, our RL training is limited to 100 steps of single-turn GRPO. We do not explore agentic RL and leave this as future work, where the policy interacts with the environment over multiple turns, which is natural next step given the long-horizon nature of the problems we synthesize.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: We introduced FrontierSmith, an automated pipeline that transforms closed-ended coding problems into open-ended ones using targeted mutations and idea-divergence filtering. Training Qwen3.5-9B and 27B on FrontierSmith-generated problems achieves performance competitive with training on human-curated open-ended data, while substantially outperforming training on closed-ended problems and generalizing strongly across benchmarks. Our analysis further shows that idea divergence serves as reliable signal of open-endedness, and that synthesized problems elicit long-horizon agent behavior comparable to humancurated tasks, with agents spending substantially more turns and tokens than on closed-ended problems. Together, these findings show that open-ended coding data can be synthesized at scale without expensive expert curation. FrontierSmith therefore provides scalable source of training data for RL on long-horizon, open-ended coding tasks.

sections.title: Acknowledgments
sections.content: We thank Youliang Yuan, Ruyi Ji, Zhifei Li, the Harbor team, the Modal team, and the Laude Institute. We also thank the Kimi team for providing access to the Kimi Code API. 10 Preprint

sections.title: Appendix
sections.content: FrontierCS Team

sections.title: A Example Synthesized Problems
sections.content: Below are two example open-ended problems produced by FrontierSmith, each mutated from different closed-ended seed. A.1 Concat Factory Compression Challenge Polycarps toy language has only two commands: 1. Literal creation (L s): Creates new variable whose value is the lowercase string (length 18). 2. Concatenation (C b): Creates new variable whose value is the concatenation of the values of previously created variables and b. Variables are numbered automatically in creation order, starting from 1. For each test case, you are given list of target strings. Your task is to output valid program that constructs every target string in some variable, while keeping the program as small as possible. Input The first line contains an integer (number of test cases). For each test case: the first line contains (number of targets), followed by lines each containing one target string. Output For each test case, output: (1) an integer (number of commands); (2) lines, each or b; (3) one line with integers r1, . . . , rq, where ri is the index of variable whose value equals the i-th target. Feasibility solution is feasible if 1 5000, every command has valid syntax, every literal has length 18 with only lowercase letters, every references previously created variables, every variables value has length at most the maximum target length, and for every i, value[ri] = ti. Objective For feasible solution, the cost is = + (cid:88) s. literal commands The baseline cost for each test case is computed by splitting each target into blocks of length at most 8 without reuse: (cid:88) = targets (cid:18) + 2 (cid:25) (cid:24) 8 (cid:19) 1 . The test-case score is = 106 min(2, B/C). The final score is the mean of over all test cases. Constraints 1 20, 1 200, 1 ti 400, Memory limit: 512 MB. sum of target lengths per test case 40,000. Time limit: 3 s. 15 Preprint Example FrontierCS Team Input: 3 targets haha, ahaha, hahahaha. Program: ha, a, 1 1, 2 3, 3 3. Variables: 1=ha, 2=a, 3=haha, 4=ahaha, 5=hahahaha. Targets matched by variables 3, 4, 5. Cost = 5 + (2 + 1) = 8. Baseline = 5 + 6 + 9 = 20. Score = 106 min(2, 20/8) = 2 106 (cap). A.2 Yae Village Defense Network The ancient village of Yae has houses, numbered 1 . . . n. House 1 is the Grand Shrine, where Sakura starts and ends every day. There are candidate bidirectional roads; road connects houses ui, vi with build cost ci and travel time di. Before day 1, Sakura may build subset of roads with total cost B. Over days, Collapse beasts attack: attack targets house hj, is active on days [aj, bj], and causes damage wj if not cleared. On each day t, Sakura starts at house 1, walks along built roads (total travel time Lt), and must return to house 1. Visiting house clears all active attacks there. The task is to choose which roads to build and one patrol per day to minimize total damage from uncleared attacks. Input One instance: n, m, D, (number of attacks), (budget), followed by road descriptions (ui, vi, ci, di), daily time limits L1, . . . , LD, and attack descriptions (hj, aj, bj, wj). Output Line 1: built road indices. Lines 2 to + 1: for each day, sequence of built road indices forming walk from house 1 back to house 1. Feasibility Built roads must have total cost B; each daily patrol must use only built roads, follow valid adjacencies, start and end at house 1, and respect the daily time limit Lt. Objective Minimize Damage = (cid:80) only attacks at house 1. For baseline damage Bdmg and your damage Dyour: not cleared wj. The baseline builds no roads and stays at house 1 every day, clearing Score = 106 min(10, (Bdmg + 1)/(Dyour + 1)). Final score is the mean over all test files. Constraints 2 400, 1 3000, 1 60, 1 4000, 1 ci, di 106, 1 109, 0 Lt 109, 1 wj 1012. Time limit: 3 s. Memory limit: 256 MB. Example 4 houses, 4 candidate roads, 3 days, 4 attacks, budget 4. Building roads 12 and 23 (cost 4) and patrolling: day 1 visits house 2 (clears attack, energy 5), day 2 visits house 3 (clears attack, energy 8), day 3 stays home (clears house-1 attack, energy 4). The attack at house 4 is missed: total damage = 6, baseline damage = 19, score = 106 (19+1)/(6+1) = 2,857,142.
